If we wish to omit the parentheses from our language in a manner which avoids
semantic ambiguity, we can adopt what is called a prefix (rather than the
standard infix) notation for expressing sentential compounds. This prefix
notation is sometimes referred to as Polish notation.

In Polish notation, we define the sentences of the language inductively as follows:

1. Any expression consisting of a single atom is an sentence.

2. If $\ph$ is a sentence then $\neg \ph$ is a sentence.

3. If $\ph$ and $\psi$ are sentences and $*$ is a binary connective, then the expression $\ast \ph
\psi$ is a sentence.

For example, in Polish notation, the sentence with the same formation tree as $(P\wedge (Q\vee R))$ is
written:

$$ \wedge P\!\vee\! Q R $$

while the sentence with the same formation tree as $((P\wedge Q)\vee R)$ is written:

$$ \vee \!\wedge\! P Q R $$

Rewrite the sentences of L$_P$ listed at the end of @sec-Lp in Polish notation.
